title: Work Items Architecture: Custom Forms and Execution Hierarchy
topic: Architecture — Work Items, Custom Forms for Execution and Viewing
summary: Three-layer custom field architecture, shared field library, template-driven forms, and execution hierarchy design for the work items system.
content:
Contents
  1. Overview
  2. Three-Layer Custom Field Architecture
  3. Shared Field Library (God List) Pattern
  4. Template-Driven Form Instantiation
  5. Core Columns vs field_values — The Filter/Sort Rule
  6. Execution Hierarchy Design
  7. XOR Constraint Pattern for Typed Parent FKs
  8. Work Item Tree Grid Rendering
  9. Decision Summary

Overview

This paper documents the architectural decisions made for the MMFFDev work items system, covering custom field storage, template-driven form assembly, and the Epic/Story/Task/Defect execution hierarchy. It serves as the authoritative technical reference for the lead developer and explains the why behind each design choice against industry alternatives.

The central constraint shaping all decisions is that padmins (product admins) must be able to compose their own field sets per artefact type without engineering intervention, while the platform must still support fast list-view filtering, sprint board queries, and large-dataset tree grids without falling back to full-table scans on variable-field storage.

Three-Layer Custom Field Architecture

The Three Tables

The execution work items system splits field storage into three layers, each with a clear, non-overlapping responsibility:

LayerTablePurpose
1 — Coreo_artefacts_execution_work_itemsFixed columns shared by all items: title, description, owner, status, priority, story_points, sprint_id, item_type, parent_id, root_feature_id
2 — Extendedo_artefacts_execution_work_items_field_valuesTemplate-driven or ad-hoc field values; each row carries field_library_id + template_id FKs
3 — Libraryo_execution_custom_field_libraryGod list of all custom fields ever defined per workspace; shared across all artefact types

Rationale

This architecture is a disciplined hybrid that avoids the worst failure modes of the three main alternatives considered:

  • Pure EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value): Flexible but destroys query performance. Every filter on a custom field requires a JOIN back to the value table with a WHERE on field_name. Aggregates (e.g., sum of story points per sprint) become multi-level sub-selects. Postgres query plans on large EAV stores are notorious for inefficient sequential scans on the values table.
  • Pure JSONB blob: Postgres JSONB with GIN indexes offers better write ergonomics and reasonable single-document queries, but cross-item aggregation (e.g., "all items where custom field X > 5") requires jsonb_each expansion and is not index-friendly for range queries. JSONB also has no schema enforcement — field types, validation rules, and label lists drift silently.
  • Per-type schema tables: Used by tools that define a finite set of work item types at compile time (e.g., one table for epics, one for stories). This breaks down the moment padmins need a field that appears on both stories and defects, or when they want the same dropdown shared across five artefact types.

The three-layer hybrid pins the high-cardinality, high-query-frequency attributes (status, priority, story_points, sprint_id) as first-class columns where they benefit from B-tree indexes and efficient planner statistics. Variable fields live in the value rows, but they are schema-governed — every value row points to a field_library_id that carries the type contract (text, number, date, select, user-ref). This prevents the silent drift problem of JSONB while retaining the flexibility of EAV.

Trade-offs and Known Costs

  • Writing a fully-populated work item requires an INSERT into layer 1, then N INSERTs into layer 2 (one per extended field). This is two round-trips unless batched. The service layer must batch field_values writes in a single statement.
  • Reading a full work item with all its field values requires a LEFT JOIN. For list views this join is omitted (core columns only); for detail views the join runs against a bounded set (single item or small page). This is manageable at any realistic scale.
  • Schema migrations for field_library changes (e.g., renaming a field type) must be applied as new rows (new library entry), not in-place updates, to avoid corrupting historical value rows that reference the old definition.

Shared Field Library (God List) Pattern

What the God List Is

o_execution_custom_field_library is the single authoritative catalogue of every custom field that has ever been defined within a workspace. It stores the field's name, type (text | number | date | select | multi-select | user-ref | url), validation constraints, default value, and workspace_id. All artefact types — work items, defects, and any future execution artefact — draw from the same pool.

Advantages

  • Reuse across artefact types: A field named "Acceptance Criteria" defined once can appear on both stories and defects without duplication. Padmins do not need to re-specify the field type and validation each time they want to add it to a new template.
  • Discoverability in the template builder UI: The padmin sees a searchable palette of all fields already created in their workspace. They drag existing fields onto a template rather than always authoring from scratch. This creates a natural library-and-reuse culture.
  • Centralised validation rules: A select field with an option list (e.g., "Severity: Low | Medium | High | Critical") has its options stored once in the library. When that field appears in five different templates, all five share the same option list. Updating the list in the library propagates everywhere.
  • Consistent field_values rows: Because every value row carries field_library_id, the backend can always resolve the type contract for a value without needing to know which template was used to collect it.

Risks and Mitigations

  • Naming collisions: If two padmins independently create fields with the same semantic purpose but different names (e.g., "Priority" vs "Urgency"), the library grows with near-duplicates. Mitigation: the field library UI must surface existing fields that match by name similarity before allowing creation of a new one. A dedup check on create (case-insensitive name + type match within workspace) is a reasonable first gate.
  • Schema drift: Library entries accumulate over months. Old fields that were removed from all templates still exist in the library (and still have historical value rows). Mitigation: a "deprecated" boolean on the library entry hides it from the padmin palette without deleting its history. Value rows referencing deprecated fields remain valid for audit purposes.
  • Global renames: Renaming a library field changes the label on every template that includes it. This is usually the desired behaviour, but must be surfaced clearly in the UI: "This field appears in 7 templates. Renaming it will update all of them."

Template-Driven Form Instantiation

Tables Involved

  • o_execution_work_item_templates — named, workspace-scoped form templates assembled by padmins; each record has a name, description, and which artefact types it applies to.
  • o_execution_work_item_template_fields — ordered field slots per template: template_id, field_library_id, position (integer), required (boolean), default_value (text).

Why Templates Win Over Schema-per-Type

A schema-per-type model (e.g., one table for story fields, another for defect fields) is only viable when the set of types is fixed and small. The moment a product team wants to distinguish "Feature Story" from "Technical Story" with a different field set, the schema-per-type model requires a migration. Templates delegate that decision to the padmin at runtime.

Template-driven instantiation also cleanly separates the definition of a form (the template) from an instance of its data (the work item + its field_values rows). This is the same principle behind how Typeform, Airtable, and Notion databases work: the schema is a first-class object that can be versioned, copied, and shared, independently of the data collected under it.

How Form Rendering Works

  1. User creates a new story. The frontend fetches the template assigned to "Story" in this workspace (or lets the user pick from available templates).
  2. The template_fields rows are fetched, ordered by position. Each slot resolves to a library field which carries the type (determines the input widget) and validation rules.
  3. The form renders: core fields first (always visible — title, description, status, priority, story_points, sprint), then template fields in position order.
  4. On save, the backend writes the core columns to work_items and the template field values to field_values with the correct template_id + field_library_id foreign keys.

Ad-Hoc Fields

A field_values row may carry a null template_id, indicating an ad-hoc field added directly to a single work item (not via a template). This supports power-user workflows where someone needs to capture a one-off attribute without modifying the shared template. The field_library_id is still required — the field must exist in the library — so type safety is preserved even for ad-hoc values.

Industry Comparison

ToolApproachPadmin Control
JiraField schemes + screen schemes per project. Fields are global; screens assemble them per issue type.High but complex; requires project admin understanding of Jira's scheme hierarchy.
LinearFixed core fields per issue type; no custom field templates. Extensions via labels only.Low; opinionated simplicity is a design choice.
HeightCustom field list per list/project; fields are local, not shared across lists.Medium; each list is isolated, no library reuse.
NotionDatabase properties as first-class objects; property types per database; no cross-database reuse natively.High for single database; cross-database reuse requires linked relations.
MMFFDevShared library + named templates assembled from library fields; templates scoped to workspace and artefact type.High and structured; library creates reuse culture, templates provide consistency.

Core Columns vs field_values — The Filter/Sort Rule

The Governing Principle

A field earns a first-class column on the work_items table if and only if it satisfies at least one of the following:

  • It appears as a filter dimension in the list view (e.g., filter by status, filter by priority).
  • It is used as a sort key in the list view or sprint board (e.g., sort by priority, sort by story_points).
  • It participates in a GROUP BY or aggregate query that runs on the item set (e.g., sum of story_points per sprint).

If a field does not meet any of these criteria, it belongs in field_values. This rule keeps the work_items table narrow and its indexes efficient.

Applied to the Current Schema

ColumnReason it is first-class
statusPrimary filter and kanban group-by dimension; every list view filters by status.
prioritySort key in backlog views; filter in sprint planning.
story_pointsSummed per sprint for velocity reporting; sorted in backlog grooming.
sprint_idFK to sprints; primary dimension for sprint board grouping; filtered constantly.
item_typeDiscriminator used in every query; drives tab filtering (epics vs stories).
parent_idSelf-referential FK for tree traversal; required for hierarchy queries.
root_feature_idDenormalised for feature-scoped queries (see Hierarchy section).
owner (user FK)Filter by assignee is universal in list views.

How Tools Handle This Distinction

Jira stores status, priority, and assignee as first-class columns in the issue table (jiraissue), even though it has a separate customfieldvalue table for extended fields. This is visible in the Jira Data Center schema: the 30-odd core columns are indexed; custom fields are fetched separately. Linear goes further — it has no custom field storage at all, only labels and estimates as extensions. The tradeoff is rigidity. Height and Notion store most fields in a property value store and compensate with aggressive caching of filter results.

The MMFFDev approach matches the Jira discipline of keeping filter/sort columns first-class while delegating everything else to field_values. This is the correct call for a system that will need to support sprint boards, velocity charts, and backlog views on potentially thousands of items per workspace.

Execution Hierarchy Design

The Four Levels

The execution hierarchy is: Epic → Story → Task / Defect → Task. The tree is capped at 3 levels of depth for practical rendering reasons (a tree grid beyond 3 levels is unusable without specialised tooling). Epics and Stories live in o_artefacts_execution_work_items with item_type = 'epic' or 'story'. Tasks and Defects exist in their own tables (with typed FK references back to their parent story or defect respectively).

parent_id Self-Referential FK vs Dedicated Hierarchy Table

The parent_id column on work_items is a self-referential FK: parent_id REFERENCES o_artefacts_execution_work_items(id). This is a clean, low-overhead pattern for shallow trees (1–3 levels). The alternative — a separate adjacency table or a closure table — is justified only when:

  • The tree is arbitrarily deep (unlimited nesting).
  • The system needs to query all ancestors or all descendants of a node in a single SQL query without recursive CTEs.
  • The tree requires non-tree (DAG) relationships (multiple parents).

None of these conditions apply to the execution hierarchy. The 3-level cap means a WITH RECURSIVE CTE to fetch a full subtree touches at most 3 levels. A self-referential FK is simpler to maintain, has no fan-out write cost, and is well understood by all SQL tooling (including ORMs). A closure table would add write amplification: every insert at level 3 would require 3 rows in the closure table, every re-parent would require mass deletes and re-inserts.

root_feature_id Denormalisation

Every execution table carries a root_feature_id column that stores the ID of the feature this item belongs to. This is denormalised — the same value could be derived by walking the parent_id chain to the feature layer — but the denormalisation is intentional and load-bearing.

Without it, every feature-scoped query (e.g., "show me all stories and tasks under Feature X") would require a recursive CTE to walk from the feature node down through all children. With root_feature_id indexed on every table, the query is a simple WHERE clause:

SELECT * FROM o_artefacts_execution_work_items
WHERE root_feature_id = $1
ORDER BY item_type, parent_id;

The denormalisation cost is a constraint: any operation that re-parents an item across features must UPDATE root_feature_id on the item AND all its descendants. This is a controlled, rare operation (re-parenting across features is an edge case) and the update is bounded by the subtree size. The invariant must be enforced in the service layer — never bypass it with a direct DB write.

Sprints

The sprints table (id, subscription_id, name, goal, start_date, end_date, status) is separate from the work items hierarchy. A sprint is a time-box container, not a hierarchy node. Items are assigned to a sprint via sprint_id on work_items; epics are never sprint-bound (they span multiple sprints). This matches the Scrum model where epics are backlog containers and sprints are time-bound slices of stories and tasks.

XOR Constraint Pattern for Typed Parent FKs

The Problem Polymorphic FKs Solve (and Create)

A Task in the execution hierarchy can be a child of either a Story or a Defect. A naive design uses a single parent_id column with a parent_type discriminator (the polymorphic FK pattern). This is application-enforced — the database has no FK constraint because the target table varies. The result is that the DB cannot prevent orphaned rows (a task referencing a deleted story), and the application must always remember to filter by parent_type.

The XOR Constraint Approach

The MMFFDev design uses two typed FK columns on the tasks table:

  • parent_work_item_id REFERENCES o_artefacts_execution_work_items(id)
  • parent_defect_id REFERENCES o_artefacts_execution_defects(id)

With a check constraint enforcing exclusivity:

CONSTRAINT tasks_parent_xor CHECK (
  (parent_work_item_id IS NOT NULL AND parent_defect_id IS NULL)
  OR
  (parent_work_item_id IS NULL AND parent_defect_id IS NOT NULL)
)

This gives the database full referential integrity on both sides. Cascade deletes work correctly for both parents. The query planner can use both FK indexes. There is no silent orphan risk.

When to Use XOR vs Polymorphic FK

SituationUse XOR typed FKsUse Polymorphic FK
Fixed, small set of parent types (2–4)YesNo
Parent set may grow indefinitelyNo (XOR constraint becomes unmanageable)Yes, with app-level enforcement
Referential integrity is criticalYesNo (not DB-enforced)
Cascade delete on parents must workYesNo (must be app-managed)
FK column count is a concernMarginal (adds 1 column per parent type)Single column regardless of parent count

For the tasks table where the parent is always one of {Story, Defect}, XOR typed FKs are clearly correct. If the system ever needs tasks to attach to a third parent type, it requires a migration to add the column and update the constraint — a deliberate, reviewable change rather than a silent runtime variation.

The polymorphic FK pattern is appropriate when the parent type set is open-ended (e.g., "a comment can be attached to any entity type"). In that scenario, the trade-off of losing DB-level referential integrity is accepted in exchange for extensibility. See the entityrefs service documentation for how MMFFDev handles that pattern elsewhere.

Work Item Tree Grid Rendering

Expandable Row Model

The tree grid renders work items as rows in a flat list with indentation driven by the nesting level. Each row carries a depth value (0 = epic, 1 = story, 2 = task/defect). Parent rows have an expand/collapse toggle. The mental model is identical to a file-tree sidebar — the data is hierarchical but the DOM is a flat list with CSS left-padding per depth level.

Key implementation constraint: the grid must never render children of a collapsed parent. Children are either fetched lazily on expand (preferred at scale) or pre-fetched and hidden in state (acceptable for small workspaces). Mixing both is the production-ready approach: eager-load the first two levels on initial view, lazy-load level 3 (tasks) on expand.

Filter-Driven Depth

When a list-view filter is applied (e.g., "show only items assigned to me"), the hierarchy must be preserved: if a story matches but its parent epic does not, the epic should still appear as a collapsed ancestor row (greyed out, or shown with a "contains match" badge). This is the "ancestor path highlighting" pattern used by Linear and Height.

Implementation: the backend returns matched items plus their ancestor chains. A typical query:

-- Fetch matching items and their ancestors (2-level max)
WITH matched AS (
  SELECT id, parent_id FROM o_artefacts_execution_work_items
  WHERE subscription_id = $1 AND root_feature_id = $2
    AND status = $3  -- filter predicate
),
ancestors AS (
  SELECT w.id, w.parent_id FROM o_artefacts_execution_work_items w
  JOIN matched m ON m.parent_id = w.id
)
SELECT * FROM matched
UNION
SELECT * FROM ancestors;

The frontend then reconstructs the tree from the flat result set using parent_id as the edge definition.

Virtualisation Considerations at Scale

At low item counts (<200 visible rows), a standard React list is sufficient. Above that, the DOM node count becomes the bottleneck on low-powered devices. Virtualisation (windowing) must be added when the workspace realistically reaches 500+ items in a single feature view.

  • Windowing library options: @tanstack/react-virtual (lightweight, headless, tree-compatible) is the preferred choice. It supports variable row heights (important if rows have inline editing or multi-line titles) and does not impose a fixed-height container requirement.
  • Tree grid and virtualisation: The key challenge is that virtualised lists render only visible rows, but expand/collapse changes the logical row count. The virtual list must be notified of row count changes on toggle. TanStack Virtual handles this via a count prop that updates reactively.
  • Scroll anchor: When a user expands a row deep in the list and children are inserted above the viewport fold, the scroll position must not jump. TanStack Virtual's scrollToIndex and sticky-item support handle this correctly.
  • Current threshold decision: Do not add virtualisation until the P95 workspace item count exceeds 300 visible rows in a single tree view. Below that, optimise query performance first (root_feature_id index, sprint_id index) before adding rendering complexity.

Decision Summary

DecisionChoice MadeKey Reason
Field storage architectureThree-layer hybrid (core columns + field_values + library)Preserves filter/sort index performance on core fields; retains padmin flexibility on extended fields
Field catalogueShared god-list per workspaceEnables reuse across templates; single source of field type contracts
Form definitionNamed templates assembled from library fieldsPadmin control without engineering; decouples schema from data instances
Filter/sort field ruleFirst-class column iff used in list filter, sort, or aggregateKeeps work_items table narrow; all filter queries use B-tree indexes
Hierarchy storageparent_id self-ref FK on work_items3-level cap makes recursive CTEs trivial; no closure table overhead
Feature-scope queriesroot_feature_id denormalised on all execution tablesEliminates recursive CTE cost on the most common query pattern
Typed parent FKsXOR constraint (parent_work_item_id + parent_defect_id)Full DB referential integrity; cascade deletes work; no silent orphans
Tree grid renderingFlat list + depth indentation; lazy-load level 3 on expandCompatible with virtualisation; minimal initial payload
Virtualisation thresholdAdd when P95 visible rows > 300 in single feature viewAvoids premature complexity; index-first optimisation until that point
